key_insight: In dilute Cu-3d alloys, room-temperature resistivity is controlled by disorder-induced band broadening on the Fermi surface, not by the density of states at the Fermi level.
engineering_relevance: This gives alloy designers a more reliable microscopic target for predicting and tuning conductivity in Cu-based materials used in practical ambient-temperature applications.
method: Compare nonmagnetic, ferromagnetic, and DLM paramagnetic states
evidence_signal: The abstract reports that experimental resistivity trends are reproduced only in the disordered local moment (DLM) description, and that resistivity correlates strongly with BSF FWHM across magnetic states.
watch_item: The conclusions are focused on dilute Cu-3d alloys at ambient temperature, so they may not transfer directly to low-temperature Kondo regimes or strongly non-dilute systems.
paper_title: Resistivity in Dilute Cu-3d Alloys Governed by Disorder-Induced Band Broadening
